1: ean tais glossais ton anthropon lalo kai ton angelon agapen de me echo: Paul begins the agape-chapter by making love the necessary condition for the validity of every spiritual gift. The 'tongues of angels' (angelon glōssai) was a Second Temple Jewish concept (cf. T. Job 48-50, where Job's daughters speak in angelic tongues) — Paul assumes this as a real category of speech. Without love (agapē), it is chalkos echon (a resounding gong) — the Corinthian competition over gifts produces sound without substance.
13: nuni de menei pistis elpis agape ta tria tauta: 'but now these three remain: faith, hope, love.' Nuni de (but now) is Paul's characteristic eschatological-temporal marker: in the present age, before the complete (to teleion) arrives. The three are the characteristic present-age virtues (cf. 1 Thess 1:3; Rom 5:1-5; Col 1:4-5). Meizōn de toutōn he agapē (the greatest of these is love): not greater because it outlasts faith and hope (though it does — in the age to come there will be sight, not faith; possession, not hope), but greater because love is the nature of God himself (1 John 4:8) and therefore the eschatological reality into which faith and hope resolve.
2: ean echō prophēteian kai eidō ta mystēria panta (if I have prophecy and understand all mysteries): the second hypothetical escalation — not just tongues but the highest intellectual spiritual gifts. Mystēria = hidden divine purposes now revealed (cf. 2:7). Ean echō pasan tēn pistin hōste orē methistanai (if I have all faith to move mountains): faith here as a specific miraculous gift (cf. 12:9), not saving faith; methistanai orē echoes Jesus's saying (Matt 17:20). Outheis eimi (I am nothing) — the total negation: without love, the spiritual superstar is ontologically empty.
3: ean psōmisō panta ta hyparchonta mou (if I give away all my possessions in pieces): psōmizō = to feed by morsels, distribute piece by piece — not simply 'give all' but a deliberate act of incremental charitable distribution. Ean paradō to sōma mou hina kauchēsomai — the textual variant is significant: kauchēsomai (that I may boast, better attested) vs. kauthesomai (that I may be burned, martyrdom reference). If kauchēsomai is correct, even self-impoverishment done for pride rather than love profits nothing. The criterion is not the act but the motive.
4: hē agapē makrothymei, chrēsteuetai (love is patient, love is kind): makrothymia = long-temper, long patience under provocation (the compound of makros = long + thymos = passion/anger). Chrēsteuetai is a NT hapax — perhaps Paul's coinage from chrēstos (useful, kind); it captures active kindness in service. Ou phūsioutai (is not puffed up): physioō = to inflate — the sixth and final use of this term in 1 Corinthians, each targeting Corinthian pride (4:6, 18-19; 5:2; 8:1; 13:4).
5: ouk aschēmonei (does not act shamefully/dishonorably): aschēmoneō from aschēmōn = unshapely, dishonorable — love does not behave in ways that violate social propriety and honor. Ou zētei ta heautēs (does not seek its own interests): the anti-self-seeking formula that opposes the Corinthian competition for status. Ou paroxizetai (is not provoked/irritated): paroxizō = to sharpen, to irritate — love does not have a hair trigger for anger. Ou logizestai to kakon (does not count the evil done to it) — logizestai = to calculate, keep accounts; love does not keep a ledger of wrongs.
6: ou chairei epi tē adikia (does not rejoice at wrongdoing): adikia = injustice, unrighteousness — the same term used for the lawsuit-behavior of 6:8. Love does not find pleasure in another's moral failure (whether in the sense of Schadenfreude or of using someone's sin as an opportunity). Sygchairei de tē alētheia (but rejoices with the truth): the syn-compound (sygchairō = to rejoice together with) — love rejoices not merely at truth but in company with it, aligning its emotions with the way things actually are.
7: panta stegei... panta elpizei... panta hypomenei (love endures all things... hopes all things... perseveres all things): stegō = to cover, to endure by covering over (the root means roof-tile — what keeps out rain). The fourfold panta (all things) is emphatic and absolute. Hypomenō = to remain under a load, to persevere without moving from under pressure. The two pairs (believes/hopes and endures/perseveres) are both cognitive-dispositional and volitional-endurance aspects of love's totality.
8: hē agapē oudepote piptei (love never falls/fails): piptō = to fall — love never collapses under any pressure or into obsolescence. Ai prophēteiai katargēthēsontai (prophecies will be abolished): katargeō = to render inoperative, to nullify — the verb used throughout Paul for the abolition of obsolete covenantal arrangements (the Law, death). The charismatic gifts are functionally temporary; love is eschatologically permanent because God is love (1 John 4:8) and the age to come is God's own life.
9: ek merous gar ginōskontes kai ek merous prophēteuontes (for we know in part and we prophesy in part): ek merous = out of the partial, from a portion — the present epistemic and prophetic condition is inherently partial, bounded by the limits of the present age. Both knowledge (gnōsis) and prophecy (prophēteia) — the two most intellectually prestigious gifts in Corinth — are equally limited in the current dispensation. No gift gives full access to divine reality in this age.
10: hotan de elthē to teleion (when the complete/perfect comes): teleios = complete, mature, fully accomplished — not 'perfect' in the modern sense of flawlessness but complete in the sense of full realization. The debate over what to teleion refers to (the NT canon? personal maturity? the eschaton/parousia?) is exegetically significant. The eschatological reading fits best: when the whole arrives, the part is obsoleted — the same logic as the childhood/adulthood analogy of v. 11 and the mirror/face-to-face contrast of v. 12.
11: hote ēmēn nēpios elaloun hōs nēpios (when I was a child I spoke as a child): nēpios = infant/young child — Paul uses this term elsewhere for spiritual immaturity (3:1: 'I could not speak to you as spiritual people but as fleshly people, as infants in Christ'). Katērgēka ta tou nēpiou (I gave up childish things) — the same katargeō verb as v. 8: the abolition of the partial when the complete arrives. The childhood-to-adulthood transition is the analogy for the current-age-to-coming-age transition.
12: blepomen gar arti di' esoptrou en ainigmati (for now we see through a mirror in a riddle/enigma): esoptron = polished metal mirror (ancient mirrors gave dim, indirect reflections); ainigma = riddle, dark/indirect saying — the LXX uses it for Num 12:8 where Moses sees God 'face to face' in contrast to the prophets who see only en ainigmati. Present spiritual sight is indirect and partial; the eschatological vision is direct: prosōpon pros prosōpon (face to face). Epignōsomai kathōs kai epegnōsthēn (I will know fully, even as I have been fully known) — the passive divine knowing precedes and will perfect the human knowing.
